title: Quinsy (Peritonsillar Abscess)
oneLiner: Peritonsillar pus collection causing severe unilateral sore throat, trismus, and “hot potato” voice; requires urgent drainage and IV antibiotics to prevent airway compromise and sepsis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction** – stridor, drooling, tripod positioning, or inability to swallow secretions → the single finding is **stridor** (inspiratory or biphasic). This is not routine quinsy; this is impending arrest – call anaesthetics+ENT immediately.
- **Descending mediastinitis** – high fever, severe neck pain extending to chest, subcutaneous emphysema, or crepitus on neck palpation → the single finding is **crepitus** (gas in tissues). This is a surgical emergency; CT neck+chest and immediate senior review.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
    - Severe unilateral sore throat (rapid onset over 2–3 days)
    - Trismus (difficulty opening mouth – hallmark)
    - “Hot potato” voice / muffled speech
    - Odynophagia (pain on swallowing) with drooling
    - Ipsilateral otalgia (referred ear pain)
- Key exam findings:
    - Unilateral peritonsillar swelling (bulging above and behind the tonsil)
    - Contralateral uvula deviation (pushed away from the abscess)
    - Erythema and oedema of the soft palate
    - Tender cervical lymphadenopathy (ipsilateral)
    - Fever (often >38°C)
    - Halitosis (foetor oris)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:
    - **Peritonsillar cellulitis** – similar presentation but no pus on needle aspiration; less severe trismus, slower progression
    - **Tonsillitis (bilateral)** – bilateral sore throat, no uvula deviation, no trismus
    - **Retropharyngeal abscess** – neck stiffness/hyperextension, drooling, may have no trismus; lateral neck X-ray shows widened prevertebral space
    - **Epiglottitis** – acute, stridor, drooling, tripod positioning, “thumbprint” sign on lateral neck X-ray; **no uvula deviation**
    - **Ludwig’s angina** – bilateral submandibular swelling, floor-of-mouth oedema, tongue elevation; **no peritonsillar bulge**
    - **Infectious mononucleosis (glandular fever)** – bilateral tonsillar exudates, severe fatigue, axillary/neck lymphadenopathy, positive Monospot
- **Splitter:** Bedside **needle aspiration** – if pus is aspirated → quinsy; if no pus → cellulitis (treat with antibiotics alone).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - Needle aspiration (18G needle, 5–10 mL syringe) – diagnostic AND therapeutic; confirm pus, send for culture and sensitivity
    - Oral exam with good light + tongue depressor (gagging may rupture – be prepared)
    - Assess airway: ask patient to open mouth fully (measure inter-incisor distance), listen for stridor, check ability to swallow secretions
- **Bloods:**
    - FBC (WCC + neutrophils), CRP (markedly elevated)
    - Blood cultures (if fever or sepsis criteria)
    - Lactate (if sepsis suspected)
    - Consider Monospot (if young adult with bilateral features)
- **Imaging:**
    - First-line: **None** – diagnosis is clinical + aspiration
    - Second-line: **CT neck with contrast** – if concern for parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal extension, descending mediastinitis, or failed drainage
- **Never-miss test:**
    - **Airway assessment** – flexible nasendoscopy by ENT if trismus > 2 cm, stridor, or red flags for airway compromise. Omission risks missed obstruction.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **Secure airway** – if stridor or impending obstruction: call anaesthetics + ENT for awake fibreoptic intubation or surgical airway; do not lay patient flat
    - **Drainage** – needle aspiration (or incision and drainage if failed) with topical anaesthetic (e.g., 10% lidocaine spray)
    - **IV antibiotics** – start after cultures (see Drugs & Doses below)
    - **IV fluids** – if unable to swallow (crystalloid, e.g., Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline 1–2 L initially)
    - **Analgesia** – IV paracetamol 1 g + consider IV morphine 2.5–5 mg PRN (avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or bleeding risk)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile and swallowing improves (then switch to oral – see below)
    - Repeat drainage if symptoms persist (consider CT-guided or surgical drainage under general anaesthetic)
    - IV fluids until oral intake tolerated
    - Reassess airway q4h (NEWS2, trismus distance, voice quality)
    - Add corticosteroids? – Consider IV dexamethasone 8 mg single dose if severe trismus/oedema (reduces swelling, but may mask failure of drainage); [check local policy for dose]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Oral antibiotics: co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS for 7 days total (or clindamycin 300 mg QDS if penicillin allergy) – only after IV course completed
    - Tonsillectomy: refer for interval tonsillectomy (6–8 weeks) if recurrent quinsy (≥2 episodes) or persistent tonsillitis
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - IV fluids: Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline – 1 L/8h, adjust for oral intake
    - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if no bleeding risk, weight > 50 kg, eGFR > 30)
    - Diet: NBM initially if airway risk; otherwise, soft/liquid diet as tolerated
    - Observations: NEWS2 2-hourly until stable, then 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- First-line intravenous:
    - **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2 g IV TDS (1.2 g = 1 g amoxicillin + 0.2 g clavulanic acid) – [check local policy for dose]
    - Or **Cefuroxime** 1.5 g IV TDS + **Metronidazole** 500 mg IV TDS – use if penicillin allergy (non-severe)
    - Severe penicillin allergy: **Clindamycin** 600 mg IV QDS + consider metronidazole if poor response
- Oral step-down:
    - Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS (or clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS)
- Duration: Total 7–10 days (IV + oral)
- Key contraindications / interactions:
    - Co-amoxiclav – avoid if penicillin anaphylaxis; caution in hepatic impairment (cholestatic jaundice history)
    - Metronidazole – avoid alcohol (disulfiram-like reaction); reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment
    - Clindamycin – risk of C. difficile diarrhoea; use with caution in renal/hepatic impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose/frequency if eGFR < 30 mL/min (e.g., 1.2 g IV once daily) – [check local policy]
    - Clindamycin: no renal adjustment needed; caution in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Failing to cover anaerobes** – quinsy is a mixed aerobic/anaerobic infection. Single-agent e.g., amoxicillin alone is insufficient; always co-amoxiclav or add metronidazole.
    - **IV co-amoxiclav given QDS instead of TDS** – double dose in 24h increases diarrhoea and C. diff risk; check frequency.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Airway obstruction (can progress rapidly)
    - Sepsis / septic shock (high fever, hypotension, raised lactate)
    - Parapharyngeal / retropharyngeal space extension (neck swelling, stiff neck, dysphagia)
    - Descending mediastinitis (severe chest pain, dyspnoea, septic shock – high mortality)
    - Aspiration pneumonia (if dysphagia + poor cough)
    - Rupture of abscess causing aspiration of pus
- **Chronic:**
    - Recurrent quinsy (2–5% overall; higher if no tonsillectomy)
    - Chronic tonsillitis (may require interval tonsillectomy)
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent with prompt drainage + antibiotics: symptoms improve within 24–48h, full recovery in 1 week.
- Mortality <1% if uncomplicated; rises to >30% if descending mediastinitis develops (requires immediate thoracotomy).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always aspirate before you treat** – draining pus is both diagnostic and therapeutic; it dramatically improves pain and speeds recovery. Don’t rely on ultrasound unless you are trained.
- **Re-aspirate if no improvement in 24h** – up to 10% will re-accumulate; patient may need incision and drainage under GA.
- **Give analgesia BEFORE drainage** – IV paracetamol + small opioid bolus makes the procedure tolerable and safer (patient less likely to gag/jerk).
- **Document airway status explicitly every shift** – measure inter-incisor distance, ask about drooling, listen for stridor.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Prescribing oral antibiotics alone** – quinsy is a walled-off pus collection; antibiotics alone will not penetrate adequately and delay definitive care.
- **Discharging patient after aspiration without reassessing swallow** – the patient may still have significant trismus and risk of airway compromise; observe for at least 2–4h post drainage.
- **Missing a parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal extension** – if neck pain, swelling below the angle of the mandible, or torticollis, do not just aspirate; get CT neck and call ENT senior.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - Difficulty breathing, stridor, or drooling worsens
    - Swelling increases or returns after initial improvement
    - Fever >38°C persists or returns >6h after drainage and analgesia
    - Unable to swallow liquids/saliva despite drainage
- **Follow-up:**
    - ENT clinic in 1–2 weeks to reassess tonsils and consider interval tonsillectomy if indicated.
    - If discharged with oral antibiotics, ensure patient completes the course (7 days total).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (ENT reg or on-call):**
    - NEWS2 ≥ 5
    - Stridor or stridor after drainage
    - Trismus worsening or < 1 cm inter-incisor distance
    - Suspected parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal extension (neck stiffness, swelling below jaw)
    - Lactate > 2 mmol/L
    - GCS < 15 from any cause
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
    - GCS ≤ 8 or airway completely obstructed (call 2222)
    - Septic shock despite fluid resuscitation (MAP < 65 mmHg after 30 mL/kg crystalloid)
    - Descending mediastinitis suspected (subcutaneous emphysema, chest pain, haemodynamic instability)

rawMarkdown:
## Quinsy (Peritonsillar Abscess)
**One-liner:** Peritonsillar pus collection causing severe unilateral sore throat, trismus, and “hot potato” voice; requires urgent drainage and IV antibiotics to prevent airway compromise and sepsis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction** – stridor, drooling, tripod positioning, or inability to swallow secretions → the single finding is **stridor** (inspiratory or biphasic). This is not routine quinsy; this is impending arrest – call anaesthetics+ENT immediately.
- **Descending mediastinitis** – high fever, severe neck pain extending to chest, subcutaneous emphysema, or crepitus on neck palpation → the single finding is **crepitus** (gas in tissues). This is a surgical emergency; CT neck+chest and immediate senior review.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
    - Severe unilateral sore throat (rapid onset over 2–3 days)
    - Trismus (difficulty opening mouth – hallmark)
    - “Hot potato” voice / muffled speech
    - Odynophagia (pain on swallowing) with drooling
    - Ipsilateral otalgia (referred ear pain)
- Key exam findings:
    - Unilateral peritonsillar swelling (bulging above and behind the tonsil)
    - Contralateral uvula deviation (pushed away from the abscess)
    - Erythema and oedema of the soft palate
    - Tender cervical lymphadenopathy (ipsilateral)
    - Fever (often >38°C)
    - Halitosis (foetor oris)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:
    - **Peritonsillar cellulitis** – similar presentation but no pus on needle aspiration; less severe trismus, slower progression
    - **Tonsillitis (bilateral)** – bilateral sore throat, no uvula deviation, no trismus
    - **Retropharyngeal abscess** – neck stiffness/hyperextension, drooling, may have no trismus; lateral neck X-ray shows widened prevertebral space
    - **Epiglottitis** – acute, stridor, drooling, tripod positioning, “thumbprint” sign on lateral neck X-ray; **no uvula deviation**
    - **Ludwig’s angina** – bilateral submandibular swelling, floor-of-mouth oedema, tongue elevation; **no peritonsillar bulge**
    - **Infectious mononucleosis (glandular fever)** – bilateral tonsillar exudates, severe fatigue, axillary/neck lymphadenopathy, positive Monospot
- **Splitter:** Bedside **needle aspiration** – if pus is aspirated → quinsy; if no pus → cellulitis (treat with antibiotics alone).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - Needle aspiration (18G needle, 5–10 mL syringe) – diagnostic AND therapeutic; confirm pus, send for culture and sensitivity
    - Oral exam with good light + tongue depressor (gagging may rupture – be prepared)
    - Assess airway: ask patient to open mouth fully (measure inter-incisor distance), listen for stridor, check ability to swallow secretions
- **Bloods:**
    - FBC (WCC + neutrophils), CRP (markedly elevated)
    - Blood cultures (if fever or sepsis criteria)
    - Lactate (if sepsis suspected)
    - Consider Monospot (if young adult with bilateral features)
- **Imaging:**
    - First-line: **None** – diagnosis is clinical + aspiration
    - Second-line: **CT neck with contrast** – if concern for parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal extension, descending mediastinitis, or failed drainage
- **Never-miss test:**
    - **Airway assessment** – flexible nasendoscopy by ENT if trismus > 2 cm, stridor, or red flags for airway compromise. Omission risks missed obstruction.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **Secure airway** – if stridor or impending obstruction: call anaesthetics + ENT for awake fibreoptic intubation or surgical airway; do not lay patient flat
    - **Drainage** – needle aspiration (or incision and drainage if failed) with topical anaesthetic (e.g., 10% lidocaine spray)
    - **IV antibiotics** – start after cultures (see Drugs & Doses below)
    - **IV fluids** – if unable to swallow (crystalloid, e.g., Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline 1–2 L initially)
    - **Analgesia** – IV paracetamol 1 g + consider IV morphine 2.5–5 mg PRN (avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or bleeding risk)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile and swallowing improves (then switch to oral – see below)
    - Repeat drainage if symptoms persist (consider CT-guided or surgical drainage under general anaesthetic)
    - IV fluids until oral intake tolerated
    - Reassess airway q4h (NEWS2, trismus distance, voice quality)
    - Add corticosteroids? – Consider IV dexamethasone 8 mg single dose if severe trismus/oedema (reduces swelling, but may mask failure of drainage); [check local policy for dose]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Oral antibiotics: co-amoxiclav 625 mg TDS for 7 days total (or clindamycin 300 mg QDS if penicillin allergy) – only after IV course completed
    - Tonsillectomy: refer for interval tonsillectomy (6–8 weeks) if recurrent quinsy (≥2 episodes) or persistent tonsillitis
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - IV fluids: Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline – 1 L/8h, adjust for oral intake
    - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if no bleeding risk, weight > 50 kg, eGFR > 30)
    - Diet: NBM initially if airway risk; otherwise, soft/liquid diet as tolerated
    - Observations: NEWS2 2-hourly until stable, then 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- First-line intravenous:
    - **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2 g IV TDS (1.2 g = 1 g amoxicillin + 0.2 g clavulanic acid) – [check local policy for dose]
    - Or **Cefuroxime** 1.5 g IV TDS + **Metronidazole** 500 mg IV TDS – use if penicillin allergy (non-severe)
    - Severe penicillin allergy: **Clindamycin** 600 mg IV QDS + consider metronidazole if poor response
- Oral step-down:
    - Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS (or clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS)
- Duration: Total 7–10 days (IV + oral)
- Key contraindications / interactions:
    - Co-amoxiclav – avoid if penicillin anaphylaxis; caution in hepatic impairment (cholestatic jaundice history)
    - Metronidazole – avoid alcohol (disulfiram-like reaction); reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment
    - Clindamycin – risk of C. difficile diarrhoea; use with caution in renal/hepatic impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose/frequency if eGFR < 30 mL/min (e.g., 1.2 g IV once daily) – [check local policy]
    - Clindamycin: no renal adjustment needed; caution in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Failing to cover anaerobes** – quinsy is a mixed aerobic/anaerobic infection. Single-agent e.g., amoxicillin alone is insufficient; always co-amoxiclav or add metronidazole.
    - **IV co-amoxiclav given QDS instead of TDS** – double dose in 24h increases diarrhoea and C. diff risk; check frequency.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Airway obstruction (can progress rapidly)
    - Sepsis / septic shock (high fever, hypotension, raised lactate)
    - Parapharyngeal / retropharyngeal space extension (neck swelling, stiff neck, dysphagia)
    - Descending mediastinitis (severe chest pain, dyspnoea, septic shock – high mortality)
    - Aspiration pneumonia (if dysphagia + poor cough)
    - Rupture of abscess causing aspiration of pus
- **Chronic:**
    - Recurrent quinsy (2–5% overall; higher if no tonsillectomy)
    - Chronic tonsillitis (may require interval tonsillectomy)

### Prognosis
- Excellent with prompt drainage + antibiotics: symptoms improve within 24–48h, full recovery in 1 week.
- Mortality <1% if uncomplicated; rises to >30% if descending mediastinitis develops (requires immediate thoracotomy).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always aspirate before you treat** – draining pus is both diagnostic and therapeutic; it dramatically improves pain and speeds recovery. Don’t rely on ultrasound unless you are trained.
- **Re-aspirate if no improvement in 24h** – up to 10% will re-accumulate; patient may need incision and drainage under GA.
- **Give analgesia BEFORE drainage** – IV paracetamol + small opioid bolus makes the procedure tolerable and safer (patient less likely to gag/jerk).
- **Document airway status explicitly every shift** – measure inter-incisor distance, ask about drooling, listen for stridor.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Prescribing oral antibiotics alone** – quinsy is a walled-off pus collection; antibiotics alone will not penetrate adequately and delay definitive care.
- **Discharging patient after aspiration without reassessing swallow** – the patient may still have significant trismus and risk of airway compromise; observe for at least 2–4h post drainage.
- **Missing a parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal extension** – if neck pain, swelling below the angle of the mandible, or torticollis, do not just aspirate; get CT neck and call ENT senior.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - Difficulty breathing, stridor, or drooling worsens
    - Swelling increases or returns after initial improvement
    - Fever >38°C persists or returns >6h after drainage and analgesia
    - Unable to swallow liquids/saliva despite drainage
- **Follow-up:**
    - ENT clinic in 1–2 weeks to reassess tonsils and consider interval tonsillectomy if indicated.
    - If discharged with oral antibiotics, ensure patient completes the course (7 days total).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (ENT reg or on-call):**
    - NEWS2 ≥ 5
    - Stridor or stridor after drainage
    - Trismus worsening or < 1 cm inter-incisor distance
    - Suspected parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal extension (neck stiffness, swelling below jaw)
    - Lactate > 2 mmol/L
    - GCS < 15 from any cause
- **Call ICU / crash team:**
    - GCS ≤ 8 or airway completely obstructed (call 2222)
    - Septic shock despite fluid resuscitation (MAP < 65 mmHg after 30 mL/kg crystalloid)
    - Descending mediastinitis suspected (subcutaneous emphysema, chest pain, haemodynamic instability)
